scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lucian uses a dialogue between Solon and Anacharsis to describe the civic and military norms of Classical Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the mid-2nd century CE (c. 165) during Lucian's floruit, as he uses the classical setting to reflect on contemporary Greek cultural identity.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text describes specific Athenian municipal penalties regarding the bearing of arms in public places.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the author's floruit (c. 165) as the dialogue presents these customs as defining features of the Athenian civic identity known to the author.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes the exclusion of women from the male-dominated public gymnasium spaces of Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the author's floruit (c. 165); the gendered division of public space was a contemporary reality for Lucian.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes the Athenian system of public honors and Panhellenic athletic participation for male youth.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to c. 165, as the Panhellenic circuit and the value of athletic honors remained relevant in the Roman-era Second Sophistic.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes the Athenian practice of prioritizing safety and fitness over lethal combat in peace-time training.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the author's floruit (c. 165); Lucian contrasts Athenian 'civilized' training with more brutal methods known in his time.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule explicitly describes a unique and famous ritual (Diamastigosis) practiced specifically by the state of Sparta.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The Spartan flogging ritual at the altar of Artemis Orthia was a major tourist attraction in Lucian's time (c. 165).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text attributes a specific pedagogical law requiring military observers at cock-fights to the Athenian state.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Lucian's floruit (c. 165), as he describes this legendary/classical Athenian law to his contemporary audience.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule refers to the imperial chairs of philosophy established by Marcus Aurelius in Athens during the Roman period.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Marcus Aurelius established these chairs in 176 CE, falling within Lucian's active career.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Discusses eligibility for the publicly funded philosophy chairs in Athens under Roman imperial administration.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the immediate controversy following the establishment of the chairs in 176 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes the physical requirements for candidates for the state-stipended philosophy positions in Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Relates to the appointment process of the imperial chairs of philosophy established in 176 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Discusses a legal defense involving physical capacity and adultery within the Roman judicial context Lucian lived in.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Lucian's floruit (c. 165) as he treats this as a known legal strategy/precedent.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle specifically describes a state of affairs that existed before the reforms of Solon.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Describes the legal condition of debt slavery existing around the turn of the 6th century BCE before it was abolished.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes the early aristocratic qualifications for the Athenian archonship prior to later democratic reforms.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Refers to the period of the Eupatrid monopoly on power in the 7th century BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle is explicitly detailing the constitutional arrangements attributed to the lawgiver Draco.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Draco's constitution is traditionally dated to his legislation in -621.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes specific wealth-based office requirements attributed to the reforms of Draco.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Draco's legislation is historically fixed at approximately -621.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes the age requirement for magistrates, which remained a standard feature of the Athenian constitution in Aristotle's time.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle wrote the Athenian Constitution during his later floruit, approximately -330.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes the graduated fine system for council members based on Solonic property classes.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle describes these classes and fines as part of the functioning or recently functioning Athenian system during his time.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes the pivotal legal reforms (Seisachtheia) enacted by Solon in Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Solon's archonship and associated reforms are traditionally dated to -594.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle describes the property classes and notes that the exclusion of Thetes from office is 'still the case' in his day.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle explicitly links these historical classes to the contemporary nomenclature of his own time (c. -330).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle cites a law 'still in force' regarding the restriction of the office of Treasurer to the highest property class.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Aristotle's floruit (c. -330) as he identifies this as an active, contemporary legal rule.
